statusLine: EU average fuel stocks sit in 'watch' territory as of March 2026, with 15 of 27 member states holding at least one fuel type below the mandatory 90-day minimum — jet fuel emerging as the most widespread vulnerability.
fullAnalysis:
EU-wide average reserves stand at 95.6 days for petrol, 88.1 days for diesel, and 87.0 days for jet fuel as of March 2026, placing the bloc in aggregate 'watch' status. While petrol stocks marginally exceed the 90-day mandatory minimum at the EU level, both diesel and jet fuel averages fall short of that threshold. These headline figures, however, mask significant dispersion across member states: a group of northern and Baltic countries — Estonia, Latvia, Ireland, Finland, and Denmark — are carrying comfortable surpluses in most categories, effectively pulling the EU average upward and concealing structural strain in Central, Southern, and Western Europe.

The country-level picture is considerably more pressured. Fifteen of 27 member states are below the 90-day minimum in at least one fuel category, with several recording critical shortfalls across all three. Romania holds only 30.2 days of diesel, Portugal 21.2 days of jet fuel, and Poland 22.5 days of jet fuel — all well below half the required minimum. Luxembourg, Austria, Italy, France, the Netherlands, and Belgium also register critical readings in multiple categories. Jet fuel stands out as the most systemically weak product: 19 of 27 member states are below the 90-day threshold for aviation fuel, including major hub economies such as Germany (59.9 days), France (65.0 days), and the Netherlands (63.2 days). This warrants close monitoring given the summer travel season now underway.

The supply context adds to the concern. Brent crude has risen 11.2% since late February 2026, from $72.48 to $80.59 per barrel, coinciding with the reported late-February Middle East escalation and subsequent strategic reserve releases by several member states. Those drawdowns are likely a contributing factor to the low stock readings now visible in the March data. If reserve releases have continued into the April–June period, the gap between EU averages and underlying physical availability may have widened further by the time of this report. Retail prices reflect this pressure selectively: petrol averages €1.771/L and diesel €1.781/L EU-wide, with notable variation from Malta's €1.34/L to Denmark's €2.39/L, partly reflecting national tax structures rather than supply conditions alone.

The overall EU position remains stable enough to avoid a systemic supply emergency at this moment, but the combination of below-minimum diesel and jet fuel averages, critical individual country readings in large economies, a rising crude price environment, and an already-tapped strategic reserve buffer leaves the system with limited headroom. Policymakers should pay particular attention to the jet fuel situation across Southern and Central Europe heading into peak summer aviation demand, and to diesel availability in Romania, Slovakia, and Poland, where road freight networks are most exposed.
keyPoints: EU diesel (88.1 days) and jet fuel (87.0 days) averages are both below the mandatory 90-day minimum as of March 2026, while petrol (95.6 days) narrowly exceeds it — placing the bloc in overall 'watch' status.

Jet fuel is the most pervasive vulnerability: 19 of 27 member states hold below-minimum jet fuel stocks, including Germany (59.9d), France (65.0d), and the Netherlands (63.2d), with Portugal (21.2d) and Poland (22.5d) in critical territory heading into peak summer aviation season.

15 of 27 member states are below the 90-day threshold in at least one fuel type; Romania (30.2d diesel), Luxembourg (38.0d petrol), and Austria (55.2d diesel) represent some of the most acute individual shortfalls.

Brent crude has risen 11.2% since late-February 2026 ($72.48 → $80.59/bbl), and strategic reserve releases linked to Middle East supply disruptions have likely contributed to the low stock levels now visible in the data — reducing the buffer available for any further drawdowns.

Northern and Baltic states (Estonia, Latvia, Ireland, Finland) are carrying significant surpluses, masking the degree of strain in Central, Southern, and Western Europe; the EU-wide average is not representative of conditions in the most exposed member states.
